topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor traced the rim of her antique pottery wheel, the smooth, cool clay a familiar anchor in the swirling chaos of her thoughts. For thirty years, wheel-throwing had been her sanctuary, a quiet, exacting art form she had perfected in solitude. Then came Leo’s first clumsy attempt, a smear of mud on a pristine piece, followed by a wide-eyed fascination that quickly curdled into something far more consuming. Now, every afternoon, the workshop smelled not just of damp earth and glaze, but of Leo’s relentless, high-pitched enthusiasm. He didn't just want to play; he demanded perfection, mimicking the precise, almost surgical movements Eleanor used to employ. Watching him try to replicate her complex forms, only to mangle them with brute force, a tight knot formed beneath her ribs. She started withholding the finer tools, letting him use the thickest, ugliest clay, watching his frustration bloom into a stubborn set in his jaw. "It needs more tension, Leo," she’d murmur, her voice clipped, the instruction laced with a subtle, cutting disapproval that always seemed to land exactly where she intended.

[story 2]

I remember the day I saw it—the miniature landscape Leo had painstakingly constructed, a tiny diorama of a mossy forest, complete with painstakingly painted lichen. It was breathtakingly detailed, a miniature testament to his focus. That day, a cold, hard resolve settled in my stomach. My own passion, the meticulous crafting of stained glass, felt suddenly dull, a faded memory compared to the vibrant, demanding energy Leo now poured into the hobby. I began subtly altering the supply cabinet. The rare, deep-hued cabochons I cherished were moved to the back, hidden behind boxes of common, pale glass. When Leo asked for the sapphire blue, my response was slow, laden with an exaggerated sigh. "Oh, those are too delicate for your current style, darling. Try the amber instead." The way his brow furrowed, the slight slump of his shoulders as he accepted the lesser material, was a satisfaction that warmed me deep down. It was a quiet redistribution of power, a subtle redirection of his intense focus toward less desirable materials.

[story 3]

The rhythmic scraping of the carving knife used to soothe me, a meditative sound against the silence of my studio. Now, that sound was punctuated by Leo’s frantic hammering, a relentless percussion against the quiet. He was obsessed with wood—not the gentle shaping I enjoyed, but the aggressive, angular cuts, the kind that demanded sheer, unbridled force. I watched him sweat, his small hands trembling with exertion, trying to achieve the sharp edges I had long mastered. A sharp, almost painful tightening gripped my throat when he failed, when the wood splintered unevenly under his pressure. I didn't offer help. Instead, I walked over, picked up the specialized chisels I kept locked away, and deliberately placed them just out of reach. I watched his face cycle through confusion, then anger, and finally, a desperate, pleading look. "You need patience, Leo," I said, my tone dangerously level, devoid of warmth. "This isn't about speed; it's about understanding the grain. Something you clearly haven't grasped yet." The silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken judgment, and it was a silence I savored.
